text: Well, it's that time of year again, dear viewer. That hazy time between Christmas and New Year when everyone's full of party food and cheap procco and not entirely sure what day it is, and every content creator on YouTube gets all reflective and misty eyed as they look back on the highs and lows of the past 12 months, mostly by recycling old reviews to get a bit more mileage out of them. Everyone except me, of course, because the drinker would never steer you wrong. Believe that. Anyway, with

all that in mind, I've done my best to sort my top picks into three main categories. The good, the bad, and the ugly. I've chosen my five best movies or shows of the year, the five worst, and the five that I consider most disappointing or just plain forgettable. And damn, was it a tough decision to make. Talk about a target-rich environment. I feel like I say this every year, but 2025 really does feel like one of the most disappointing years ever in movie history. The summer movie

season saw a string of flops and underperformers. Nothing really standing out and capturing the public imagination. Cape [ __ ] movies continued their sad descent into cultural irrelevance. Franchises continue to get strip mine for everything they're worth. And even TV delivered some absolute clangers that are going to live on in infamy. >> I'm [clears throat] going to be a bad. [laughter] But let's begin on a positive note with my five favorites of 2025, shall we? And

top of the list has got to be F1, the movie. Not because I think it's the best or the most artistically worthy or any of that [ __ ] but because I just enjoyed it so [ __ ] much. I actually came out of the movie theater feeling good about myself, feeling inspired and elated by what I'd just seen. This was the ultimate feel-good sports movie for me. A classic story of an agent pro who never quite got his chance coming back for one last shot at glory and having to

mentor a brash young upstart along the way. Packed with likable characters, superb racing sequences, and driven by a commanding performance by Brad Pitt, who seemed to have stopped aging at some point in his mid-40s, it's one of those rare films today that doesn't treat the older generation as laughable relics of a less enlightened time who need to be ridiculed and forgotten, or as disposable stepping stones to elevate their younger and better replacements.

It's just a fun, sincere, action-packed sports drama. And if you missed it when it was out in theaters, I would absolutely recommend it over the holidays. And I still can't believe I'm saying this, but the number two spot goes to a Star Wars show. Oh man. Oh god. Oh man. I know it's practically heresy in this day and age, but what can I say? Season 2 of Andor was absolutely superb TV. movie level production values, mature storytelling, compelling characters, and great performances all

came together into a season finale that justified every penny spent on it. Andor is the kind of project we'll probably never see again from Lucasfilm. A distinctly mature, thoughtful, and complex take on a Star Wars universe that's leaning more and more towards cutesy aliens, idiotic characters, and formulaic nostalgia bait storytelling. Andor had the balls to be something very different. It didn't rely on lightsabers or CGI Luke Skywalker to get you invested. Instead, it told a really

[ __ ] good story that demanded you pay attention and rewarded you when you did. It wasn't for everyone, that's for sure, and it definitely made you work for it, but the payoffs were absolutely worth the effort. The number three spot on my list goes to Warfare, an A24 movie from the start of the year that tells the story of an American unit caught up in a brutal firefight during the Iraq War. It's a distinctly unholy kind of movie. sharp, brutal, and unforgiving. There's

no glamour or moving speeches or cool hero shots in this one. Just guys desperately fighting to stay alive, risking their own lives to help their buddies in trouble and trying their best to sort through the chaos and confusion of war. It's gritty, it's unpredictable, and it's a [ __ ] excellent war movie. My fourth pick surprised even me, but the more I think about it, the more I decided, yeah, [ __ ] it. This movie was a lot of fun, and it deserves a spot on

the list. So, gather around my little soda popups as we talk about K-pop demon hunters. On the surface, it should be just another forgettable puff piece animated movie with some catchy musical numbers. But look, beneath the flashy spectacle and the hyper energetic animation, and K-pop Demon Hunters is actually a pretty decent story about loyalty, friendship, discrimination, and past mistakes coming back to haunt us. Is it high art? No. But it is fun and compelling stuff

driven by a trio of goofy, sharply drawn, and likable main characters that you can't help but warm to. I might not be the target audience for it, but [ __ ] me, it got me anyway. Last up is Galmo Del Toro's Frankenstein, an excellent remake of the classic Mary Shelly novel. Let's be honest, Frankenstein's a story that's been retold a million times from every possible angle, so you'd think a modern-day remake is the last thing weed needs. But damn, was it good. Del Toro's

distinct visual style marries up perfectly with the iconic story of a man trying to play God. And combined with excellent performances by a superb cast, the end result is a dark, poignant fairy tale of ambition, obsession, twisted experiments, revenge, and betrayal. The story of Frankenstein has always been a haunting one, full of questions about what it means to play God, and what our own creations might think of us. But I don't think any version stuck with me

quite like Del Toro's. I do also have to give a few honorable mentions to films that I really enjoyed but didn't quite make the list. Sinners, Weapons, and The Smashing Machine were all strong contenders and definitely worth the time if you haven't seen them yet. Sadly though, it's not been all good news for 2025. In fact, I'd argue this was one of the shittiest years in living memory for movies. So, narrowing down my worst projects of the year to just five was no

easy task. But here goes anyway. My first pick has got to be Alien Earth. I went into it with high hopes and even kind of defended it after watching the first episode, but holy [ __ ] was my faith misplaced with this one. Every episode was like a descent into a [ __ ] nightmare. Every character somehow managing to be dumber and more unlikable than the last. It's like the script was written by HAL 9000 after Dave Bowman starts disconnecting all his logic circuits. None of it made a lick

of sense. And what's so disappointing is that they clearly dumped a lot of money into this thing. It looks great. It looks like the world of Alien. And that is where the similarities end. Trying to fuse the story of Peter Pan and the Lost Boys with the Alien franchise and throwing in a dose of corporate espionage for good measure was a bold move indeed, but it absolutely didn't pay off for them. Making your central characters intentionally stupid only works if the script around them is

actually smart. And well, this one wasn't. By the end, I was genuinely laughing out loud at how ridiculous it had all become. And to this day, I do not know how the actors made it through their scenes without losing their minds. As for entry number two, well, we're not [ __ ] around now. We're girl bossing the hell out of this one with my second pick, G20. Now, imagine you were tasked with writing a parody of all the ridiculous tropes of the girl boss action movie genre, the implausible

fight scenes, the power fantasy protagonist who never fails at anything, the open hatred of men and everything they represent, the forced diversity and the social justice lectures, and then some [ __ ] decided to make it into an actual movie, but played it 100% serious. Well, then you get G20, a movie where an almost 60-year-old Viola Davis plays the president of the United States and has to fight her way through an army of mercenaries single-handed to take down a wannabe crypto bro and save the

world from AI deep fakes, right? I am not even close to kidding with this one. Now, I like Viola Davis. She's a good actor with real presence and gravitas, but one thing she isn't is an action star. And watching her struggling through fight scenes with stunt men half her age, twice her size, and probably three or four times her strength isn't just funny, it's actually kind of embarrassing. I don't know who was working harder on this film. Davis just to get through the action set pieces

without Keeling over or the stunt team to make her look so that she could even do a fraction of this stuff for real. G20 got laughed out of the room and rightly so because it was a complete joke of a movie. Now, I honestly couldn't decide if this next one belonged in the bad or the ugly category, but the latter would suggest I had some kind of expectation for it, and that would be a lie. So, here it is. Predator: Badlands, or as I like to call it, the fabulous emancipation of Lumpy

the Yaoucha from the shackles of toxic masculinity. I don't really know what I expected to get out of this movie, but it definitely wasn't a predator sitting around a campfire with his quirky android buddy talking about his feelings while a cute animal sidekick looks on. This absolute cinematic abortion was hailed by idiots like Grace Randolph as a bold reinvention of the Predator franchise that would finally appeal to women and LGBT people. As if women and LGBT people would ever

watch a Predator movie expecting a story about relationships and personal dramas. It was absurd and insulting. The Android was arguably the most annoying sidekick of the entire year, and it completely misunderstood the appeal of the Predator franchise. Either that or it just did not give a [ __ ] in the first place. Releasing it theatrically was a massive mistake because everyone got to see how much of a box office flop it really was. And honestly, I am glad that it failed

because it absolutely deserved to. Now, no list of terrible projects this year would be complete without Last of Us season 2. Possibly the most predictable of all failures, but somehow managing to outdo even itself with a story and character that were, if possible, even worse than the shitty game it was based on. A story of survival, revenge, and tested loyalties in a brutal post-apocalyptic world reduced to a tright, vomitinducing teenage romance between two characters that absolutely

nobody could ever care about. It's not often I can say a project was made worse by the absence of Pedro Pascal, but The Last of Us absolutely was taking away the training wheels and horribly exposing Bella Ramsay's complete lack of acting ability, charisma, charm, screen presence, and likability. Everyone pretty much knew she wouldn't be able to carry a show like this on her own, and they were right. Every episode was a slow tedious pretentious self-important hour of retardation to

get through. And by the end, I was rooting for the [ __ ] fungus zombies. It did give us one of the single worst lines paired up with the worst delivery in TV history, though. >> I'm going to be a dad. >> Yeah. My last pick was as inevitable as a hangover on New Year's Day. Star Trek Section 31 was a strong candidate for one of the worst movies ever made. Never mind the worst movie of 2025. It is without a doubt the worst film ever shot out by the Star Trek franchise. And I'm

including the J.J. Abrams movies in that. Part heist movie, part revenge story, part space adventure, and all complete ass. It's like Taikoiti's idea of Star Trek if you performed a frontal lobotomy on the guy. A cast of cartoonish morons led by Michelle Yo, who doesn't seem to know why the [ __ ] she's there or what even's going on, and decides to chew the scenery with the kind of unbridled haminess that would make a pantoime villain blush. It's a film that feels like it was made purely

to spite the old Star Trek fan base, what's left of it, at least. Like Alex Curtsman and his buddies know that everyone hates them now. They know their time's coming to an end, and so they set out to do as much damage as possible with shite like this. Whether it's the tedious revenge storyline that's been done a million times before in Modern Trek, or the terrible acting, the insufferable characters, the ugly cheap set design, or the laughable action scenes. Every single aspect of this film

feels like it was flung together in a single afternoon by people whose knowledge of Star Trek consists of about 15 minutes surfing Wikipedia. It was a disaster in every sense of the word. A complete waste of a hundred million dollars. And outside of trying to [ __ ] a meat grinder, it was probably one of the worst ways to spend 95 minutes of your life. Oh yeah, and you're probably thinking to yourself right now, drinker, what about Snow White? Surely that deserves a spot on your list of worst

films of the year. Well, dear viewer, some movies transcend the very concept of good and bad to become their own special category of cinematic experience. And Snow White and the seven CGI abominations is such a case. A film that was mocked and [ __ ] on from its very inception. Dogged by controversies, PR disasters, and desperate re-shoots and course corrections, all trying to fix something that was fundamentally broken from the outset. It opened to horrendous reviews, lost hundreds of

millions of dollars for the studio, put the brakes on further liveaction remakes, for now at least, nuked Rachel Ziggler's career from orbit, and even derailed Galadot's momentum. But hey, it made for great YouTube content. So, on behalf of content creators everywhere, please accept this lifetime achievement award for biggest LCA movie of the year. You earned it, Snow White. >> It's Hollywood, baby. >> Yes. Yes. >> This final category belongs to the most disappointing, forgettable, or downright

pointless movies of 2025. And in a way, this was probably the hardest to narrow down because I'm pretty sure I could have filled at least 20 spots with this [ __ ] But let's try to be discerning, shall we? The first one was an obvious pick. James Gun Superman by James Gun. A movie that was so overhyped and overpromoted that people were tired of the [ __ ] thing before it even came out. They really pulled out all the stops with this one. They bombarded us with teasers, trailers, clips,

interviews, and at the center of it all, James Gun himself, who just couldn't shut the [ __ ] up about this movie. Unfortunately for him, the film itself was the very definition of mid, weighed down by a bland and uninspiring protagonist who clearly wasn't James Gun's creative focus. A meandering plot riddled with holes and inconsistencies, goofy attempts at irreverent comedy that usually failed to land, and a growing realization that James Gun can really only do one kind of movie, Guardians of

the Galaxy. Superman was his big shot, his chance to launch the brand new DCU, and he fumbled it big time. Now, the only reason I even remember this second movie existed is because I trolled through my old reviews of the year and suddenly realized, "Oh, yeah, Electric State was a thing, wasn't it?" I mean, I didn't think it was possible for a film of similar budget to have even less cultural impact than Avatar, but somehow this misfiring wannabe sci-fi fantasy

epic found a way. Long, tedious, and grindingly slow paced with a pair of lead actors who have all the chemistry of a bowl of tepid water. A Millie Bobby Brown who's so desperate to escape the childhood role that made her famous that she's speedrunning her way towards middle age. It's a movie that doesn't seem to know what it wants to be or even why it exists. And after slogging through two hours of [ __ ] dialogue and terrible action scenes, I've got no idea either. This second movie was a

difficult pill to swallow because on the face of it, Mickey 17 looked like a promising sci-fi action movie with a wickedly satirical streak made by the director of Parasite, and with a strong cast that included Robert Patson, Tony Klette, and Mark Ruffalo. What we got instead was a painfully unfunny comedy that even Patson couldn't salvage, a fragmented and borderline incoherent plot about lone sharks, deep space colonization, a love triangle that goes nowhere, a lecture about immigration

that nobody asked for, and possibly the most on then SNL reject impression of Donald Trump the world has ever seen. Whatever magic allowed Bong Jun Ho to make Parasite, it absolutely didn't translate into big budget sci-fi action movies. The film flopped hard. everyone forgot about it like a week later and the world kept on turning like nothing happens. Now, I can't say I've ever been a huge fan of the Jurassic World movies. In fact, I'd say that each of them has

managed to sink to even greater depths of retardation and creative bankruptcy, but I did at least hold out some kind of hope that they might have learned their lesson with Rebirth, clearly intended to jumpst start a third trilogy of these movies. Nope. If anything, it's even worse than what came before. A story that manages to be both crushingly derivative and mind-bendingly nonsensical with a group of supposedly intelligent characters who manage to make the worst possible decisions in

every situation and a plot that hinges entirely on a Snickers rapper getting sucked into an air vent. I am not even kidding. It's a movie that actually made me sad when I think how this franchise started out 30 years earlier. Like running into your old high school English teacher only to find out that he's a drunken hobo begging for change now. It made money, sure, but nowhere near as much as its predecessors. And part of me wonders if a new trilogy is even going to be viable if they continue

on this kind of trajectory. Now, if ever there was a movie that highlighted just how far the MCU has fallen, it was Captain America: Brave New World. A film that completely lost confidence in itself and what it was doing, got chopped up and reshot multiple times in a desperate attempt to try to please everyone, and ended up satisfying no one. Marvel made their intentions clear with Sam Wilson's character in Falcon and the Winter Soldier, a shamelessly race baiting social justice struggle

session that spent most of its time lecturing its own audience and pushing the message so [ __ ] hard that it ended up breaking itself. I'm willing to bet they were fully prepared to double down on all that [ __ ] with Brave New World, only to realize it was about as popular as a spunk flavored lollipop and desperately tried to course correct midway through production. The result was a sliced up Frankenstein's monster of a film made up of characters and story arcs from like three different

movies. Plot points come and go faster than internet memes. Characters are introduced and then dropped without warning. And major story elements fizzle out into nothing or get brought in suddenly with no buildup. It really was the biggest mess that Marvel have ever put out. And when I think about it, it was the cherry on the cake of shitty, underwhelming superhero movies this year. I've talked many times about the decline of the genre ever since Endgame, but 2025 really was the year that proved

it beyond any doubt. Thunderbolts, Brave New World, Fantastic 4, and Superman, they all had their shot, and they all failed to recapture the magic of 10 years ago. Yeah, I'm sure Avengers Doomsday next year is going to make money, but only because they've lured back every single actor from previous generations with massive paychecks. And at the end of the day, people are still suckers for nostalgia. Unfortunately, that's a card you can really only play once. More broadly though, I think 2025

is going to be remembered as the beginning of the end for the theatrical experience. It's something that's been on the cards for a long time. And for all its faults, I do think the hype and the fandom of big franchises like the MCU actually kept interest in theatrical movies alive beyond its natural time. But even that couldn't hold back the change forever. Movie theaters are dying out and 2025 just confirmed it. I'm sure you'll still get the occasional mega hit

from time to time, but the sad fact is that people just aren't going to the movies as much anymore. Films don't generate the kind of hype and excitement they used to, and I'd expect that trend to continue into 2026. Get ready for a summer movie season of lowered expectations and desperate media spin because it's coming. Believe me. On a more personal note, though, as much as 2025 hasn't been a great year for movies, it's been a [ __ ] awesome one for me. This has been by far my biggest

and most successful year ever on YouTube. I've had the chance to travel the world, go to conventions, and meet loads of awesome people, attend movie premieres, and even had the chance to make more short films. And all of this has been made possible by you guys, by your continued support and your belief in me and what I do here. And for that, I'm never going to stop being grateful. So, as 2025 winds down, the drinker shall raise a glass to all of you because you earned it, my friends.
Anyway, that's all I've got for this year. Go away now.
